passage.text:
IELTS Reading Practice
Arts & Culture
10 Reading Passages  ·  500–600 words each  ·  Level: B1 (IELTS 3.5–5.5)

Contents
Passage 1: Music — Why Do We Love It?
Passage 2: Cinema — The Art of Storytelling on Screen
Passage 3: Museums — Keeping the Past Alive
Passage 4: Street Art — From Vandalism to Fine Art
Passage 5: Cultural Heritage — Protecting What Matters
Passage 6: Architecture — Buildings That Tell a Story
Passage 7: The Importance of Cultural Diversity
Passage 8: Literature — The Power of Stories
Passage 9: Traditional Festivals Around the World
Passage 10: The Art of Photography — Capturing the World

Passage 1: Music — Why Do We Love It?
Topic: Music & Human Experience  |  Level: B1 (IELTS 3.5–5.5)
Paragraph A
Music is one of the most universal forms of human expression. Every culture in the world, throughout all of recorded history, has had some form of music. People use music to celebrate, to mourn, to tell stories, to pray, and simply to enjoy themselves. Babies respond to music before they are even born — studies show that newborns prefer music they heard while still in the womb. Most people have experienced the powerful effect that music can have on their emotions — a song can make you feel happy, sad, excited, or calm within seconds. Scientists have spent many years trying to understand why music affects us so strongly, and what they have found is both fascinating and surprising.
Paragraph B
When people listen to music they enjoy, the brain releases a chemical called dopamine — the same chemical released when we eat something delicious or spend time with people we love. This is why music can feel genuinely pleasurable. Research has also shown that music has a strong connection to memory. A song you heard during an important moment in your life can bring back vivid memories and strong emotions, even many years later. This is why music is often used with patients who have memory problems — even people who have difficulty remembering recent events can often remember songs from their youth. Music also affects our physical state — upbeat music can increase our heart rate and energy levels, while slow, calm music can help us relax and even sleep better.
Paragraph C
Music also plays an important social role. It brings people together. At concerts, festivals, and sporting events, music creates a shared experience that can make strangers feel connected to each other. Singing together — in choirs, at religious services, or simply around a campfire — has been shown to release chemicals in the brain that promote feelings of bonding and trust. Research suggests that groups of people who make music together feel more cooperative and connected than groups that do not. Music is also a powerful part of cultural identity. Traditional songs, folk music, and national anthems connect people to their heritage and to each other. In many cultures, music plays a central role in important rituals — weddings, funerals, religious ceremonies, and festivals.
Paragraph D
Learning to play a musical instrument has benefits that go well beyond music itself. Studies show that children who learn to play an instrument develop better language skills, stronger memory, and higher levels of concentration compared to children who do not. Learning music also teaches patience, discipline, and the ability to deal with failure and keep practising. These skills are useful in all areas of life. Playing music has also been shown to reduce stress and anxiety in both children and adults. Some hospitals and care homes now use music therapy — using music to help patients recover from illness, manage pain, or improve their emotional wellbeing. Researchers believe that music engages almost every area of the brain at once, making it one of the most complete mental workouts possible.

Passage 2: Cinema — The Art of Storytelling on Screen
Topic: Film & Cinema  |  Level: B1 (IELTS 3.5–5.5)
Paragraph A
Cinema is one of the most popular forms of entertainment in the world. Every year, billions of people go to the cinema or watch films at home on streaming services. Films tell stories in a powerful and immediate way, combining moving images, sound, music, and dialogue to create experiences that can make audiences laugh, cry, think, and feel deeply. The history of cinema began in the 1890s, when inventors in France and the United States developed the first machines to capture and show moving images. The earliest films were very short and had no sound. Sound was added to films in the late 1920s, and this changed cinema completely, opening up new possibilities for storytelling.
Paragraph B
Making a film is a very complex and collaborative process that involves many different people with different skills. The director is responsible for the overall creative vision of the film — they guide the actors and the camera operators to create the look and feel they want. The screenplay writer creates the story and the dialogue. Producers manage the money and organisation of the production. Camera operators, lighting technicians, costume designers, makeup artists, and set designers all work together to create what appears on screen. After filming is finished, film editors put the footage together in the right order, and sound engineers add music and sound effects. A typical Hollywood film can take several years to make and can cost hundreds of millions of dollars.
Paragraph C
Hollywood — the film industry based in Los Angeles, California — has dominated global cinema for most of the twentieth century and into the twenty-first. American films are watched by audiences in almost every country in the world, and English has become the most common language of international cinema. However, other film industries have also produced world-famous and critically important films. Indian cinema — particularly the Hindi-language industry based in Mumbai, which is often called "Bollywood" — produces more films per year than Hollywood and has a massive audience across South Asia and among Indian communities worldwide. South Korean cinema has gained enormous international recognition in recent years, particularly after the film "Parasite" won the Oscar for Best Picture in 2020 — the first non-English language film to do so.
Paragraph D
The way people watch films has changed dramatically in recent years. For most of the twentieth century, going to a cinema was the main way to see a new film. Today, streaming services like Netflix, Amazon Prime, and Disney+ allow people to watch thousands of films instantly at home on their television, computer, or smartphone. This has been very convenient for viewers, but it has caused problems for traditional cinemas, many of which have seen the number of visitors fall significantly. Some filmmakers and critics worry that streaming is changing the art of cinema — that films designed to be watched on a small screen at home are made differently from films designed for a large cinema screen. The debate about the future of cinema continues as technology keeps changing the way we experience films.

Passage 3: Museums — Keeping the Past Alive
Topic: Museums & Cultural Heritage  |  Level: B1 (IELTS 3.5–5.5)
Paragraph A
Museums are places where objects from history, art, science, and culture are collected, preserved, and displayed for the public. There are thousands of museums around the world, ranging from tiny local history museums in small towns to enormous international institutions like the British Museum in London, the Louvre in Paris, and the Smithsonian in Washington DC. Museums play an important role in society. They help us understand who we are, where we come from, and how people have lived throughout history. They preserve objects that might otherwise be lost or destroyed. They educate people of all ages and backgrounds. And they provide a space for reflection, wonder, and learning that is different from anything available online or in books.
Paragraph B
Over the past few decades, museums have worked hard to become more accessible and welcoming to a wider audience. Many large museums now offer free entry — the British Museum and the National Gallery in London, for example, are free to all visitors. Audio guides and information in multiple languages make it easier for international visitors to enjoy and understand the collections. Interactive displays and hands-on activities have been introduced to engage younger visitors and make learning more fun. Many museums also have dedicated education programmes for school children, with workshops and activities linked to the school curriculum. Online collections and virtual tours allow people who cannot visit in person to explore museums from anywhere in the world.
Paragraph C
One of the most debated issues in the museum world today is the question of cultural ownership — who has the right to hold and display objects from different cultures. Many of the world's most famous museums, including the British Museum and the Louvre, hold large collections of objects that were taken from other countries during the period of European colonialism in the 18th and 19th centuries. Countries including Greece, Nigeria, and Egypt have asked for important cultural treasures — such as the Elgin Marbles from Greece and the Benin Bronzes from Nigeria — to be returned. Supporters of return argue that these objects belong to the people and cultures that created them. Opponents argue that large international museums can preserve and display them to a much wider global audience.
Paragraph D
Technology is changing the way museums operate and how visitors experience them. Digital technology allows museums to create detailed digital records of their entire collections, making them easier to study and share with researchers worldwide. Augmented reality (AR) technology — which adds digital information or images to what you can see in real life — can bring museum exhibits to life in new ways. For example, visitors might point their phone at an ancient statue and see what it looked like when it was first painted thousands of years ago. Some museums are experimenting with fully virtual experiences, where visitors can explore a digital museum from their own home. While some people worry that technology will reduce the special experience of visiting a museum in person, others believe it will make museums more engaging and relevant to modern audiences.

Passage 4: Street Art — From Vandalism to Fine Art
Topic: Visual Art & Urban Culture  |  Level: B1 (IELTS 3.5–5.5)
Paragraph A
Street art is art that is created in public spaces — on walls, pavements, bridges, and other surfaces in towns and cities. It includes graffiti, murals, stencil art, and installations. For many years, street art was seen mainly as vandalism — illegal damage to property — and people who created it were treated as criminals. Artists could be arrested and fined for painting on walls without permission. However, attitudes towards street art have changed significantly in recent decades. Today, street art is exhibited in galleries, sold for large sums of money, and recognised as a serious and important art form. Cities around the world actively encourage street artists to create works in public spaces, as it is seen as a way to make cities more beautiful and interesting.
Paragraph B
One of the most famous street artists in the world is Banksy, a British artist whose real identity remains a mystery. Banksy began creating stencil-based graffiti in Bristol, England, in the 1990s. His work often has a political or social message — commenting on war, poverty, consumerism, and social inequality in a way that is both funny and thought-provoking. Banksy's images have appeared on walls across the world, from London and New York to Gaza and Paris. Although his work is illegal — created without permission — it has become hugely popular and is now considered valuable art. In 2018, one of his paintings sold at auction for over one million dollars, and then famously destroyed itself by passing through a shredder hidden in the frame moments after the sale.
Paragraph C
Street art and graffiti have their roots in the hip-hop culture that developed in New York City in the 1970s. Young people from poor areas of New York — particularly the Bronx — used spray paint to write their names or "tags" on walls, trains, and public spaces as a way of expressing themselves and marking their presence in the city. This evolved over time into more complex and artistic styles. The style spread to cities across the United States and then around the world. Today, what began as a form of rebellion by young people with few resources has become an internationally recognised art movement, with some former graffiti artists showing their work in major galleries and selling it for very high prices.
Paragraph D
Many cities now commission street artists to create large murals on public buildings, and some areas have become famous tourist attractions because of their street art. The Shoreditch area of London, the Wynwood Walls in Miami, and the Hosier Lane in Melbourne are well-known examples of places where large collections of street art draw visitors from around the world. Street art can also be used to communicate important social messages. During political protests and social movements, murals and street art have been used to express solidarity, raise awareness, and call for change. However, the line between art and vandalism is still debated. Not everyone who paints on a public wall is creating something that improves the space — and the right to decide what goes on public property is an ongoing area of disagreement.

Passage 5: Cultural Heritage — Protecting What Matters
Topic: Heritage & Preservation  |  Level: B1 (IELTS 3.5–5.5)
Paragraph A
Cultural heritage refers to the traditions, buildings, objects, and places that a society passes down from one generation to the next. It includes physical things — such as ancient buildings, statues, paintings, and historical documents — as well as things that cannot be touched, such as traditional music, dances, languages, and food customs. Cultural heritage is important because it connects people to their history and to each other. It gives communities a sense of identity and belonging. It helps people understand where they came from and what makes their culture unique. Heritage is also economically important — historic buildings, archaeological sites, and cultural traditions attract tourists and generate significant income for many countries.
Paragraph B
Despite its importance, cultural heritage faces many threats. Natural disasters such as earthquakes, floods, and fires can destroy irreplaceable buildings and objects in a very short time. Armed conflict is one of the greatest dangers to cultural heritage — wars have destroyed ancient cities, temples, and artworks throughout history. In recent years, militant groups have deliberately destroyed important historical sites in Iraq and Syria as a form of cultural attack. Pollution and climate change also damage heritage sites — rising sea levels threaten coastal archaeological sites, while air pollution is eating away at the stone of ancient buildings and statues. Uncontrolled tourism can also cause damage — millions of visitors to sites like the Colosseum in Rome or the temples of Angkor Wat in Cambodia gradually wear down fragile structures.
Paragraph C
To protect cultural heritage around the world, the United Nations created UNESCO — the United Nations Educational, Scientific and Cultural Organization. UNESCO maintains a World Heritage List — a list of places that are considered to be of outstanding importance to humanity as a whole. The list currently includes over 1,100 sites in more than 160 countries, ranging from the pyramids of Egypt and the Great Wall of China to the Galápagos Islands and the old city of Dubrovnik. Sites on the list receive international recognition and can apply for funding to help protect and maintain them. Countries also have their own national laws and organisations to protect important buildings, monuments, and traditions.
Paragraph D
Preserving cultural heritage in a changing world requires creativity and modern technology. Digital technology is now playing a very important role in conservation. Researchers can create detailed three-dimensional digital records of buildings and objects, so that even if they are destroyed, a perfect digital copy is preserved. Drones and satellite images are used to monitor heritage sites and detect damage or illegal activity. Traditional skills and craft techniques — such as stone carving, weaving, and pottery making — are being taught to new generations of craftspeople to keep these traditions alive. However, preservation is not just about stopping things from changing. Many experts argue that heritage must also be "living" — relevant and meaningful to the communities whose history it represents, not just preserved as a relic of the past.

Passage 6: Architecture — Buildings That Tell a Story
Topic: Architecture & Design  |  Level: B1 (IELTS 3.5–5.5)
Paragraph A
Architecture is the art and science of designing and constructing buildings. Every building — from a small house to a great cathedral or a modern skyscraper — is a piece of architecture. Great architecture does more than provide shelter and space. It can inspire, surprise, and move people emotionally. It can express the values and ambitions of the society that created it. Famous buildings like the Eiffel Tower in Paris, the Taj Mahal in India, the Sydney Opera House in Australia, and the Colosseum in Rome are not just useful structures — they are works of art that have come to represent the cultures and cities where they stand. Architecture tells us a great deal about the people who built it — their technology, their beliefs, their resources, and what they considered beautiful.
Paragraph B
Throughout history, different periods have been associated with distinctive architectural styles. Ancient Greek architecture, with its elegant columns and symmetrical design, was designed to convey order, beauty, and reason — values that the Greeks considered most important. Gothic architecture, which flourished in medieval Europe from the 12th to 16th centuries, is characterised by tall pointed arches, large stained glass windows, and soaring stone towers — all designed to reach towards heaven and inspire a feeling of awe. The Baroque style that followed was much more dramatic and decorative, with curved shapes, rich ornamentation, and a sense of movement. In the twentieth century, the modernist movement rejected historical decoration and aimed instead for clean lines, simple forms, and the use of new industrial materials like concrete, steel, and glass.
Paragraph C
Modern architecture faces a very different set of challenges from those of the past. One of the most pressing issues today is sustainability — designing buildings that use as little energy as possible and have a minimal impact on the environment. Buildings account for around 40% of all energy consumption globally, so making them more energy-efficient is extremely important in the fight against climate change. "Green buildings" use features such as thick insulation, solar panels, green roofs covered in plants, and systems that collect and reuse rainwater. Some architects are designing buildings that produce as much energy as they consume — these are called "net zero" buildings. The challenge is to make sustainable buildings affordable and attractive enough that they become the norm rather than the exception.
Paragraph D
Technology is also transforming the way buildings are designed and built. Computer software now allows architects to create extremely detailed three-dimensional models of their designs before a single brick is laid. This makes it possible to identify and solve problems at the design stage, saving time and money during construction. New materials — including stronger and lighter versions of concrete and steel, and innovative materials like transparent wood — are giving architects new creative possibilities. 3D printing technology is beginning to be used to construct small buildings and architectural components. Some experts predict that in the future, robots and automated machines will carry out a much larger proportion of construction work. As all these technologies develop, the work of architects will change significantly — but the goal of creating buildings that are functional, beautiful, and suited to the needs of those who use them will remain the same.

Passage 7: The Importance of Cultural Diversity
Topic: Culture & Society  |  Level: B1 (IELTS 3.5–5.5)
Paragraph A
Cultural diversity means having many different cultures, languages, traditions, and ways of life within a society or community. The world is home to thousands of different cultures, each with its own history, language, food, music, customs, and beliefs. Cultural diversity exists both between countries — Japan and Brazil, for example, have very different cultures — and within countries, where different ethnic groups, religious communities, and regional traditions live side by side. In the modern world, globalisation and migration have brought people from different cultural backgrounds into closer contact than ever before. Many countries, particularly in North America, Western Europe, and Australia, have become home to people from dozens or even hundreds of different cultural backgrounds.
Paragraph B
Cultural diversity brings many benefits to societies. When people from different backgrounds come together, they bring different experiences, skills, and ways of thinking. This variety can lead to greater creativity and innovation — research suggests that diverse teams tend to come up with more creative solutions to problems than groups where everyone has a similar background. Culturally diverse cities and societies also benefit from a wider range of food, music, art, and cultural events, which enrich everyone's life. International businesses often find that having employees from many different cultural backgrounds helps them better understand and serve customers around the world. Many great works of art, literature, and music have been created when people from different cultures have come together and influenced each other.
Paragraph C
However, cultural diversity can also create challenges. When people from different cultures live in the same community, there can be misunderstandings, tensions, and sometimes conflict, particularly over differences in values, religious practices, or social customs. Some people feel threatened by changes in the culture of the place where they grew up, and worry that their own traditions will be lost as their society becomes more diverse. Immigration can become a politically sensitive issue when people disagree about how many newcomers a country should accept and how well they are integrating into society. Discrimination and prejudice — judging people unfairly because of their cultural background, religion, or ethnicity — remain serious problems in many countries.
Paragraph D
Many countries have adopted policies to manage cultural diversity in positive ways. Multicultural policies aim to recognise and respect the different cultural backgrounds of all citizens, rather than expecting newcomers to give up their own culture and fully adopt the majority culture. Schools can play an important role by teaching children about different cultures and histories, helping them develop respect and empathy for people who are different from themselves. Celebrating cultural diversity — through festivals, food, art, and cultural exchange programmes — helps communities appreciate and enjoy the different backgrounds and traditions of their members. Most researchers and social scientists agree that the key to making diversity work well is encouraging both respect for different cultures and a shared commitment to common values such as equality, fairness, and respect for human rights.

Passage 8: Literature — The Power of Stories
Topic: Literature & Reading  |  Level: B1 (IELTS 3.5–5.5)
Paragraph A
Literature — writing that is considered to have artistic value — has been an important part of human culture for thousands of years. Long before people could write, stories were passed on by word of mouth from one generation to the next. Some of the oldest written works in existence — such as the Epic of Gilgamesh from ancient Mesopotamia, written around 4,000 years ago — are stories. Literature includes many different forms: novels, short stories, poetry, plays, and essays. Great literature can entertain, but it also does much more than that. It makes us think about the big questions of life — who we are, how we should live, and what makes us human. It helps us understand people and experiences that are very different from our own.
Paragraph B
Reading literature has many benefits for individuals. Numerous studies have found that people who read a lot of fiction tend to have stronger empathy — the ability to understand and share the feelings of others. This is because reading a novel requires us to imagine ourselves inside the mind of a character, seeing the world through their eyes. This practice of imagining other people's thoughts and feelings transfers to real life, making readers more understanding and compassionate in their relationships with others. Reading also improves vocabulary, writing skills, and general knowledge. It reduces stress — research shows that just six minutes of reading can lower the heart rate and reduce muscle tension significantly. And reading before bed has been shown to improve sleep quality.
Paragraph C
Literature also reflects and shapes the society in which it is created. Great novels can change the way people think about important social issues. For example, Charles Dickens' novels in the nineteenth century drew public attention to the terrible conditions faced by poor children in Victorian England and helped build support for social reforms. Harriet Beecher Stowe's novel "Uncle Tom's Cabin," published in 1852, is widely believed to have strengthened opposition to slavery in the United States. More recently, novels like "The Handmaid's Tale" by Margaret Atwood and "1984" by George Orwell have encouraged millions of readers to think carefully about power, freedom, and social control. Literature gives writers a platform to challenge accepted ideas and encourage society to look at itself honestly.
Paragraph D
Despite the many benefits of reading, literacy rates and the amount of time people spend reading books have declined in many countries. The rise of digital technology — social media, video streaming, and online entertainment — means that people have more ways to spend their leisure time than ever before, and books must compete with many other attractive options. Some people worry that young people in particular are reading fewer books than previous generations. However, e-books and audiobooks have made reading more accessible and convenient for many people. Book clubs — groups of people who read the same book and meet to discuss it — have grown in popularity in many countries, both online and in person. Many educators and parents continue to emphasise the importance of developing a reading habit from a young age, believing that literature remains one of the most valuable gifts we can give to children.

Passage 9: Traditional Festivals Around the World
Topic: Festivals & Traditions  |  Level: B1 (IELTS 3.5–5.5)
Paragraph A
Every culture in the world has its own festivals and celebrations. Festivals are special times when communities come together to mark important events — the changing of seasons, religious occasions, harvests, historical moments, or the achievements of the past. They are often joyful, colourful events filled with music, dancing, food, and special rituals. Festivals play an important role in society. They strengthen community bonds by bringing people together in shared celebration. They pass on cultural traditions and values from one generation to the next. They give people a break from everyday routines and a chance to enjoy life. And they are often powerful expressions of a community's identity, history, and beliefs.
Paragraph B
Some festivals are celebrated by people around the world, regardless of their cultural background. New Year celebrations take place in almost every country, though the date and the way it is celebrated vary enormously. Christmas is celebrated by Christians in many countries, but it has also become a cultural event observed by many non-Christians in countries around the world. Diwali — the Hindu festival of lights — is one of the most widely celebrated festivals in the world, with colourful celebrations not only in India and Nepal but also in Indian communities in the United Kingdom, the United States, Canada, and Australia. The Islamic festival of Eid al-Fitr, which marks the end of the holy month of Ramadan, is celebrated by Muslims in countries across the globe.
Paragraph C
Many fascinating festivals reflect the unique history and traditions of specific cultures. The Rio Carnival in Brazil is one of the world's largest and most spectacular celebrations, with enormous parades featuring elaborate costumes, samba music, and dancing in the streets. The Holi festival in India — also known as the festival of colours — involves people throwing brightly coloured powder and water at each other to celebrate the arrival of spring and the victory of good over evil. The Day of the Dead in Mexico is a time when families honour and remember their ancestors, building colourful altars decorated with flowers, photographs, and favourite foods of the deceased. The Songkran festival in Thailand marks the traditional Thai New Year and involves joyful water fights in the streets.
Paragraph D
In many countries, traditional festivals have been affected by modernisation and globalisation. As people's lives become more urban and fast-paced, some traditional festivals have lost their original meaning and become more commercial celebrations. At the same time, globalisation has spread some festivals to new countries and audiences. Halloween, which has its roots in an ancient Celtic festival, is now celebrated in countries all over the world where it was not previously known. However, many communities are making active efforts to preserve and revive traditional festivals that were in danger of disappearing. Local governments, cultural organisations, and community groups often play an important role in keeping these traditions alive, recognising that festivals are an essential part of cultural identity and social cohesion.

Passage 10: The Art of Photography — Capturing the World
Topic: Visual Art & Photography  |  Level: B1 (IELTS 3.5–5.5)
Paragraph A
Photography is the art and practice of capturing images using a camera. Since its invention in the 19th century, photography has changed the way humans see and understand the world. Before photography, the only way to record a person's appearance or an important event was through painting or drawing — a slow and expensive process. Photography made it possible to capture a precise image of reality in a fraction of a second. Today, photography is everywhere. Billions of photographs are taken every day around the world — most of them on smartphones. Images are shared on social media, used in advertising, journalism, science, and art. Photography is now one of the most common forms of human expression and communication.
Paragraph B
Photography was invented in the early 19th century. The first permanent photograph was taken in 1826 or 1827 by French inventor Joseph Nicéphore Niépce. In the following decades, the process was improved and made faster. By the late 19th century, photography had become popular enough for ordinary people — not just professional photographers — to take photos. George Eastman, an American businessman, founded the Kodak company in 1888 and introduced affordable cameras that anyone could use. In the 20th century, colour photography became widely available, and the invention of the digital camera in the 1970s eventually led to the transformation of photography we know today — where everyone carries a high-quality camera in their pocket.
Paragraph C
Photography has many uses beyond personal memories and artistic expression. In journalism, photographs provide evidence of important events — from wars and natural disasters to political movements and sporting achievements. The power of a single photograph to change public opinion has been demonstrated many times throughout history. Photographs taken during the Vietnam War in the 1960s and 70s, for example, shocked the world and helped turn public opinion against the conflict. In science, photography is an essential research tool — microscopes and telescopes use cameras to capture images of things too small or too far away for the human eye to see. In medicine, X-rays and scan images help doctors diagnose and treat illness. In architecture and design, photographs help professionals communicate and evaluate their work.
Paragraph D
Digital technology has transformed photography completely. Digital cameras and smartphones make it free and instant to take hundreds of photographs, whereas film photography required buying and developing rolls of film. Photo editing software like Adobe Photoshop allows photographers to adjust colours, remove unwanted elements, and even dramatically change an image. This has raised questions about honesty and trust — if photographs can be so easily altered, how can we know whether an image shows what really happened? "Deepfakes" — images and videos created using artificial intelligence that appear to show real events or people but are actually fake — have made this problem even more serious. Despite these concerns, photography remains one of the most powerful and universal forms of communication and art in the modern world.
